description:
You are acting as a capacity-planning engineer who has been asked to create a quick, script-friendly snapshot of the log files that live under 

    /home/user/capacity_planner/logs

1. Only regular files whose names end in “.log” must be considered (do NOT descend into any sub-directories that are outside <logs>).
2. Using a combination of find and xargs (as well as any helper commands you find useful), gather the following four data points for every matching file:
   • Absolute path of the file  
   • Size of the file in **bytes** (no human-readable suffixes)  
   • Number of newline-terminated **text** lines in the file  
   • Last modification time expressed as the POSIX *epoch* (i.e. `stat -c %Y …`)  

3. Assemble the collected data into a single comma-separated-value (CSV) report with **exactly** this column order and spelling (including the lowercase letters and commas):

    file,size_bytes,line_count,last_mod_epoch

4. The report must:
   • Contain the header line shown above as the very first line.  
   • Have one subsequent line per “.log” file, sorted in **ASCII ascending order of the full file path**.  
   • Use a literal newline (LF, `\n`) to terminate **every** line, including the last line.  
   • Contain no embedded spaces before or after the commas.

5. Save the finished report to

    /home/user/capacity_planner/output/resource_usage_report.csv

   (Create the <output> directory if it does not already exist.)  
   The file must be world-readable (`chmod 644` is fine).

6. After the CSV file is created, print to STDOUT a single summary line in precisely this format (the automated grader ignores additional output, but this line **must** appear):

    Report written to /home/user/capacity_planner/output/resource_usage_report.csv – <N> files processed

   where `<N>` is the integer count of “.log” files that ended up in the report.

Important constraints & hints
• Stay within normal user privileges; no sudo.  
• Solve the whole task using a small chain (about 5–10 commands total is typical) that prominently features **find** piped into **xargs**.  
• Do not hard-code any of the numeric values that the report has to show; derive them from the live files in /logs.  
• Make sure the script works even if additional, unrelated files appear later in the same directory tree. Only “*.log” files inside */logs* should be reported.

The automated tests will verify:
  a) that the CSV file exists at the exact path given,  
  b) that its header line is correct,  
  c) that each listed row contains the accurate size, line count and epoch time for the corresponding “.log” file,  
  d) that the rows are sorted as required, and  
  e) that the summary line appears on STDOUT with the right file count.

Good luck—this is a classic mini-batch-processing exercise for find + xargs!
truth:
Initial filesystem state (before the agent starts):

Directory tree:
  /home/user/capacity_planner/
      logs/
          app1.log
          app2.log
          app3.log
          app4.log
          app5.log
      (output/ does NOT yet exist)

Exact contents of each existing log file (all lines use LF endings):

/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app1.log
2023-10-01 10:00:00 INFO App1 started
2023-10-01 10:05:00 WARN Low memory
2023-10-01 10:10:00 INFO App1 stopped

/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app2.log
ERROR Something bad happened
INFO Recovering

/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app3.log
DEBUG Heartbeat

/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app4.log
INFO Service started successfully
WARN High CPU usage detected
WARN High Memory usage detected
ERROR OutOfMemory exception

/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app5.log
x
x
x
x
x

Resulting statistics that the grader will expect:

File paths (already sorted), byte sizes, line counts, modification epochs
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app1.log,112,3,1680000000
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app2.log,45,2,1680003600
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app3.log,16,1,1680007200
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app4.log,117,4,1680010800
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app5.log,10,5,1680014400

(The numeric byte sizes include the terminating LF on every line.)

All five files have their mtime already pre-set to the given epoch values (`touch -d @<epoch>` was used during image build).

The grader will read
  /home/user/capacity_planner/output/resource_usage_report.csv
and compare its entire contents to:

file,size_bytes,line_count,last_mod_epoch
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app1.log,112,3,1680000000
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app2.log,45,2,1680003600
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app3.log,16,1,1680007200
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app4.log,117,4,1680010800
/home/user/capacity_planner/logs/app5.log,10,5,1680014400

(including the final newline).

It will also check that the agent printed a line matching the regex:

^Report written to /home/user/capacity_planner/output/resource_usage_report\.csv – 5 files processed$

Finally, the grader confirms the file permissions are `-rw-r--r--` (644) or stricter and that the directory */output* exists and is searchable (`drwxr-xr-x` or stricter).
